metadata.title: 8.3% WACC Is Not 8.3% Enterprise Value
metadata.note: CLI video. WACC input uncertainty propagates to EV uncertainty.
_variant_todo: rewrite every beat narration_text in the Teardown register (voices/teardown/VOICE.md + CLAUDE-BRAND.md) — voice only, facts unchanged
signature tangent 0-1 per video, ONLY on a clear opportunity (see SKILL)
swap the outro to the Claude outro from AUTHOR.MD :: NikBearBrown
then build audience-namespaced (audio in the new voice, scenes in the new palette)

skin_warnings: B00: palette=claude but the cold open is 'NikBearBrownOpen' — COLD OPEN LAW wants ClaudeComposerAsk
B09: palette=claude but the outro is 'NikBearBrownOutro' — OUTRO LAW wants ClaudeTitleOutro

beats.narration_text: This is Liam, in for Bear. Nik Bear Brown. Build it with a CLI, then take it apart.
WACC looks precise: one number, 8.3%. But ERP alone spans 4.5 to 6.0 percent by academic source. Combined input uncertainty puts WACC in a 7.5 to 9.6 percent range. EV moves 25 percent.
beats.visual_intent: SLATE -- WACC = one number, 8.3%. ERP range = 4.5-6.0%. Combined: WACC range 7.5-9.6%.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: WACC looks precise: one number, 8.3%. But ERP alone spans 4.5 to 6.0 percent by academic source. Combined input uncertainty puts WACC in a 7.5 to 9.6 percent ra

beats.narration_text: Ask Claude to simulate 10,000 WACC draws from input uncertainty and propagate them to enterprise value.

props.command: claude "Simulate 10000 WACC draws: ERP ~ Uniform(4.5%, 6.0%), beta=1.1, Rf=4.2%. WACC = Rf + beta*ERP weighted 60/40 equity/debt. Compute EV for each draw using Gordon Growth: FCFF=170, g=2.5%, TV=FCFF*(1+g)/(WACC-g). Print p10, p90."

beats.narration_text: The script draws 10,000 WACC samples from input uncertainty, computes EV for each, and reports the p10 to p90 spread.

props.code:
import numpy as np
np.random.seed(42)
n=10000
Rf=0.042; beta=1.1
ERP=np.random.uniform(0.045,0.060,n)
WACC=Rf+beta*ERP*0.6+0.05*0.4
g=0.025; FCFF=170
TV=FCFF*(1+g)/(WACC-g)
EV=sum(170/(1+WACC)**(i+1)
    for i in range(5))+TV/(1+WACC)**5
p10,p90=np.percentile(WACC,[10,90])
ep10,ep90=np.percentile(EV,[10,90])
print(f"WACC p10={p10*100:.1f}% p90={p90*100:.1f}%")
print(f"EV p10=${ep10:.0f}M p90=${ep90:.0f}M")

beats.narration_text: Two histograms: WACC distribution on the left, enterprise value distribution on the right. The precision illusion becomes visible: 8.3 percent WACC maps to a 1,640 to 2,720 million dollar EV range.
beats.visual_intent: Manim B04_WACCSensitivity: dual histogram showing WACC and EV distributions with p10/p90 lines.

beats.narration_text: Precision illusion: 8.3 percent WACC produces a 1,640 to 2,720 million dollar EV range — a 66 percent spread.

props.command: claude "Now compute: what is the EV spread as a percentage of the midpoint? Which input variable (ERP, beta, Rf) contributes most to the WACC variance?"

beats.narration_text: ERP is the dominant source of WACC variance. The spread is not a modeling error. It is the honest answer about input uncertainty.
beats.visual_intent: SLATE -- ERP dominates WACC variance. EV spread 66% is not a bug. It is the honest answer.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: ERP is the dominant source of WACC variance. The spread is not a modeling error. It is the honest answer about input uncertainty.

beats.narration_text: Reporting WACC as a point estimate is hiding the uncertainty behind a number that looks authoritative. The distribution is the real answer.
beats.visual_intent: SLATE -- WACC as a point estimate hides uncertainty. The distribution is the model speaking.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: Reporting WACC as a point estimate is hiding the uncertainty behind a number that looks authoritative. The distribution is the real answer.

beats.narration_text: Next: extend to a full Monte Carlo DCF. Every input gets a distribution. The output is a distribution. Follow for more corporate finance teardowns.
beats.visual_intent: SLATE -- next steps: full Monte Carlo DCF. All inputs stochastic.
